From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Town where stores and housing are owned by one company that is the main employer

For the 2016 documentary film, see [Company Town (film)](/wiki/Company_Town_(film) "Company Town (film)").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1d/Siedlung_eisenheim.jpg/330px-Siedlung_eisenheim.jpg)](/wiki/File:Siedlung_eisenheim.jpg)

The town of [Siedlung Eisenheim](/w/index.php?title=Siedlung_Eisenheim&action=edit&redlink=1 "Siedlung Eisenheim (page does not exist)") in [Oberhausen](/wiki/Oberhausen "Oberhausen"), Germany

A **company town** is a place where all or most of the stores and housing in the town are owned by the same company that is also the main employer. Company towns are often planned with a suite of [amenities](/wiki/Amenity "Amenity") such as stores, houses of worship, schools, markets, and recreation facilities.

Some company towns were established to improve living conditions for workers, but many have been regarded as controlling and/or exploitative.[[1]](#cite_note-1) Others were not planned, such as [Summit Hill, Pennsylvania](/wiki/Summit_Hill,_Pennsylvania "Summit Hill, Pennsylvania"), United States, one of the oldest, which began as a [Lehigh Coal & Navigation Company](/wiki/Lehigh_Coal_%26_Navigation_Company "Lehigh Coal & Navigation Company") mining camp and mine site nine miles (14 km) from the nearest outside road.

## Paternalism

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Company_town&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Paternalism")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/57/Bayreuthburg01.JPG/330px-Bayreuthburg01.JPG)](/wiki/File:Bayreuthburg01.JPG)

Company town *[Burg](/w/index.php?title=Burg_(Bayreuth)&action=edit&redlink=1 "Burg (Bayreuth) (page does not exist)")* in [Bayreuth](/wiki/Bayreuth "Bayreuth"), Germany

Main article: [Welfare capitalism](/wiki/Welfare_capitalism "Welfare capitalism")

[Paternalism](/wiki/Paternalism "Paternalism"), a subtle form of [social engineering](/wiki/Social_engineering_(political_science) "Social engineering (political science)"), refers to the control of workers by their employers who seek to force middle-class ideals upon their working-class employees. Many nineteenth-century business people considered paternalism as a moral responsibility, or often a religious obligation, which would advance society while furthering their business interests. Accordingly, the company town offered a unique opportunity to achieve such ends.

Although many prominent examples of company towns portray their founders as "capitalists with a conscience", for example, [George Cadbury](/wiki/George_Cadbury "George Cadbury")'s [Bournville](/wiki/Bournville "Bournville"), if viewed cynically, the company town was often an economically viable ploy to attract and retain workers. Additionally, for-profit shops within company towns were usually owned by the company, which was unavoidable to its isolated workers, thus resulting in a monopoly for the owners.[[2]](#cite_note-Crawford-2)

Although economically successful, company towns sometimes failed politically due to lacking elected officials and municipally owned services.[[3]](#cite_note-Garner_a-3) Accordingly, workers often had no say in local affairs, and therefore felt dictated to. Ultimately, this political climate caused resentment amongst workers and resulted in many residents losing long-term affection for their towns. Such was the case at the company town of Pullman, Chicago in the 1890s.

## History of American company towns

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Company_town&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: History of American company towns")]

### Pullman lesson

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Company_town&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Pullman lesson")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/73/Greenstone_and_Arcade_Pullman.jpg/330px-Greenstone_and_Arcade_Pullman.jpg)](/wiki/File:Greenstone_and_Arcade_Pullman.jpg)

Pullman in the late 19th century

Although many small company towns existed in mining areas of [Pennsylvania](/wiki/Pennsylvania "Pennsylvania") before the [American Civil War](/wiki/American_Civil_War "American Civil War"), one of the most significant and most substantial early company towns in the United States was [Pullman](/wiki/Pullman,_Chicago "Pullman, Chicago"), developed in the 1880s just outside the Chicago city limits. The entirely company-owned town provided housing, markets, a library, churches, and entertainment for the 6,000 company employees and an equal number of dependents. Employees were not required to live in Pullman, although workers tended to get better treatment if they chose to live there.

The town operated successfully until the economic [panic of 1893](/wiki/Panic_of_1893 "Panic of 1893") when demand for the company's products declined, and Pullman lowered employee wages and hours to offset the decrease in demand. Despite this, the company refused to lower rents in the town or the price of goods at its shops, thus resulting in the [Pullman Strike](/wiki/Pullman_Strike "Pullman Strike") of 1894. A national commission formed to investigate the causes of the strikes found that Pullman's paternalism was partly to blame and labeled it "Un-American".[[4]](#cite_note-4) The report condemned Pullman for refusing to negotiate and for the economic hardships he created for workers in the town of Pullman. "The aesthetic features are admired by visitors, but have little money value to employees, especially when they lack bread." The State of Illinois filed suit, and in 1898, the [Supreme Court of Illinois](/wiki/Supreme_Court_of_Illinois "Supreme Court of Illinois") forced the Pullman Company to divest ownership in the town, which was annexed to Chicago.[[5]](#cite_note-Archaeology-5)

However, government observers maintained that Pullman's principles accurately provided his employees with a [quality of life](/wiki/Quality_of_life "Quality of life") otherwise unattainable. Still, they recognized that his excessive paternalism was inappropriate for a large-scale corporate economy and thus caused the town's downfall. Accordingly, government observers and social reformers alike saw the need for a balance between control and well-designed towns, concluding that a model company town would only succeed if independent professionals, acting as a buffer between employers and employees, took a role in conception, planning, and management of these towns.[[6]](#cite_note-6)

Historian Linda Carlson argues that the managers of corporate towns in the early 20th century believed they could avoid the mistakes made by George Pullman in the 1880s. She says they:

:   wanted to create a better life for their employees: decent housing, good schools, and a "morally uplifting" society. In return, they expected stable, hard-working employees who would eschew the evils of drink and, most importantly, not fall prey to the blandishments of union organizers.[[7]](#cite_note-7)

Thus, the Pullman Strike did not kill the company town concept but rather initiated a new chapter in their existence. Over the next thirty years, the old model of paternalism was abandoned in favour of new professionally designed company towns with architects, landscape architects, and planners translating "new concepts of industrial relations and social welfare into new physical forms". This suited capitalists of the day who were keen to avoid the experiences of Pullman. The first real example occurred at [Indian Hill-North Village, Massachusetts](/wiki/Indian_Hill-North_Village,_Massachusetts "Indian Hill-North Village, Massachusetts"), in 1915.[[2]](#cite_note-Crawford-2): 45

### Decline of American company towns

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Company_town&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Decline of American company towns")]

As access to surrounding municipalities increased, residents of company towns gained access to an increasing amount of government-funded public resources such as schools, libraries, and parks. Accordingly, there was no longer a need for the amenities of company towns which, before [welfare capitalism](/wiki/Welfare_capitalism "Welfare capitalism"), had previously been unattainable to the working class.[[2]](#cite_note-Crawford-2)

This was well received by some employers as the idea of 'laissez-faire' individualism, which promoted entrepreneurial virtues of hard work being rewarded rather than direct charity, began to shape new-age paternalism.[[2]](#cite_note-Crawford-2)

The Roosevelt administration's [New Deal](/wiki/New_Deal "New Deal") dealt the final blow to end American company towns by raising minimum wages, encouraging industrial self-governance, and pushing for the owners of company towns to "consider the question of plans for eventual employee ownership of homes".[[2]](#cite_note-Crawford-2): 204

## Model company towns

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Company_town&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Model company towns")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/69/Houses_on_Greendale_Avenue%2C_Port_Sunlight.jpg/250px-Houses_on_Greendale_Avenue%2C_Port_Sunlight.jpg)](/wiki/File:Houses_on_Greendale_Avenue,_Port_Sunlight.jpg)

An example of houses at [Port Sunlight](/wiki/Port_Sunlight "Port Sunlight"), England

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ae/Houses%2C_Bournville_2.JPG/250px-Houses%2C_Bournville_2.JPG)](/wiki/File:Houses,_Bournville_2.JPG)

Houses in [Bournville](/wiki/Bournville "Bournville"), England

See also: [Model village](/wiki/Model_village "Model village")

During the late nineteenth century, model company towns materialized as enlightened industrialists recognized that many poor workers were living in appalling conditions.[[8]](#cite_note-parsons-8) These industrialists wished to combat the unsanitary and congested conditions common to working-class districts to create better living conditions for workers.[[9]](#cite_note-Garner_b-9): xi Model company towns such as [Port Sunlight](/wiki/Port_Sunlight "Port Sunlight") (1888) and [Bournville](/wiki/Bournville "Bournville") (1895) were influential in regards to their building and planning innovation. The ideas generated from these model towns are regarded as having a significant influence on the [Garden City movement](/wiki/Garden_city_movement "Garden city movement").[[3]](#cite_note-Garner_a-3)[[10]](#cite_note-Jackson-10)

The model company town is concerned with creating a productive and prosperous company. Enlightened industrialists believed this could be achieved by providing a healthier residential environment for their employees. Planning a model company town involved the fusion of new notions of house design and layout.[[11]](#cite_note-gaskell-11): 440 The paternalism of the enlightened industrialist was exhibited in his desire to provide an environment for his employees that was aesthetically appealing and which included well-designed residences, parks, schools, libraries, and meeting halls.[[3]](#cite_note-Garner_a-3): 4 The industrialist also wished to contribute to his workers' well-being by providing social programs such as sporting events and functions.[[3]](#cite_note-Garner_a-3) This, however, highlights the power and immense control possessed by the company owner, who could shape the lifestyle and activities of his employees to serve his interests and those of the company.[[9]](#cite_note-Garner_b-9)

### Model company towns in Britain

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Company_town&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Model company towns in Britain")]

Model villages for agricultural workers were founded in the early 19th century in the United Kingdom. The creation of model company towns was particularly evident in Britain during the latter half of the nineteenth century with the creation of [Saltaire](/wiki/Saltaire "Saltaire") (1851), [Bournville](/wiki/Bournville "Bournville"), [Port Sunlight](/wiki/Port_Sunlight "Port Sunlight"), [Creswell](/wiki/Creswell,_Derbyshire "Creswell, Derbyshire") and [New Earswick](/wiki/New_Earswick "New Earswick") (1901) and coincided with the housing-reform movement, which emphasized the improvement of housing for the working class.[[8]](#cite_note-parsons-8) These model towns contrasted with the overcrowded conditions in British working-class districts, which were often characterized by congested housing, unsanitary conditions, and poor provision of open space and facilities.[[11]](#cite_note-gaskell-11) Model company towns promoted the idea of orderly, planned town development as well as the notion of preparing for the needs of the community to provide healthier living conditions.[[12]](#cite_note-cherry-12)

Model company towns around the mid-nineteenth century, such as Copley (1849), near [Halifax](/wiki/Halifax,_West_Yorkshire "Halifax, West Yorkshire"), and Saltaire (1853), close to [Bradford](/wiki/Bradford "Bradford"), had improved dwellings for workers, which contrasted with working-class housing in other industrial villages and cities.[[9]](#cite_note-Garner_b-9): 87 These model company towns prompted the creation of others, such as Port Sunlight, Bournville, and Creswell, within an environment of reform.[[9]](#cite_note-Garner_b-9)

[Port Sunlight](/wiki/Port_Sunlight "Port Sunlight") (1888) in Cheshire was established by [William Hesketh Lever (later Lord Leverhulme)](/wiki/William_Lever,_1st_Viscount_Leverhulme "William Lever, 1st Viscount Leverhulme") of Lever Brothers – a soap and tallow manufacturer. The earlier layout of this model company town was planned to suit the site's undulating topography.[[10]](#cite_note-Jackson-10): 47 Port Sunlight catered for the Lever Brothers employees with improved housing (cottages of varying designs and materials) and gardens, as well as social and community facilities, including an auditorium, a school, tennis courts, and bowling greens.[[10]](#cite_note-Jackson-10)[[12]](#cite_note-cherry-12) Port Sunlight combined the use of formal and informal planning elements, such as straight streets close to the town centre and curved streets in the residential areas. This combination of the formal and informal represented a new feature of British town planning.[[13]](#cite_note-13)

[Bournville](/wiki/Bournville "Bournville") (1895), near Birmingham, was established by the Cadbury brothers, [George](/wiki/George_Cadbury "George Cadbury") and Richard. George and Richard Cadbury chose to transfer the Cadbury factory to this new site to provide their employees with improved living conditions and a country environment that they could enjoy – a far cry from Birmingham's busy, smoky city centre. The firm provided education as a compulsory academic course, and workers were allowed to complete commercial or technical training. The Cadburys also encouraged their workers to get involved in the social life of Bournville by providing sports facilities, athletic and cultural clubs, and social events such as summer parties. [George Cadbury](/wiki/George_Cadbury "George Cadbury"), a Quaker, preached Christian values, such as respectability, thrift, and sobriety, and sought to unify the Bournville community through rituals such as gift-giving between employer and employee. The firm also established work councils, such as the Women's Works Council, and supported trade unions.[[14]](#cite_note-dellheim-14)

Bournville represented the union of industry and nature[*[clarification needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Please_clarify "Wikipedia:Please clarify")*] as the company town boasted the attractiveness of the countryside and low-density development with well-built and visually appealing dwellings.[[12]](#cite_note-cherry-12)[[14]](#cite_note-dellheim-14) Unlike Port Sunlight, Bournville catered for a mixed community, where residences were not restricted to the workforce only.[[10]](#cite_note-Jackson-10): 49 Bournville illustrated how, towards the end of the nineteenth century, low-density development was being punctuated[*[clarification needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Please_clarify "Wikipedia:Please clarify")*] along with the provision of open air, space, and sunlight.[[12]](#cite_note-cherry-12): 317 Bournville's gardens, parks, tree-lined streets, sense of spaciousness, and country setting enhanced its aesthetic appeal and demonstrated George Cadbury's endeavour to provide workers with a healthy, beautiful, and well-ventilated environment.[[10]](#cite_note-Jackson-10): 49

The [Bolsover Company](/wiki/Bolsover_Colliery_Company "Bolsover Colliery Company") developed two exemplary [mining communities](/wiki/Pit_village "Pit village") in Derbyshire during the late nineteenth century: [Bolsover](/wiki/Bolsover "Bolsover") (1891) and [Creswell](/wiki/Creswell,_Derbyshire "Creswell, Derbyshire") (1896). The Bolsover Company aimed to provide improved living conditions for the miners and their families in these model industrial villages. The houses at Creswell were built in concentric circles, and within these circles was a large open parkland and a bandstand.[[11]](#cite_note-gaskell-11): 446 Not only did the Bolsover Company aim to provide better housing, but they also wished to improve workers' moral fibre, believing that the provision of facilities and the promotion of workers' welfare would discourage drunkenness, gambling, and bad language. The Bolsover Company provided facilities deemed beneficial for employees at both villages, including clubhouses, bowling greens, cooperative society stores, cricket pitches, and schools.[[11]](#cite_note-gaskell-11) During the early years of these model industrial villages, the Bolsover Company organized various events intended to enhance community life, such as flower shows, lectures, sporting events, concerts, teas, and dances.[[11]](#cite_note-gaskell-11): 447

## Examples (by country)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Company_town&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Examples (by country)")]

### Belgium

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Company_town&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Belgium")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/87/B%C3%A2timents_du_Grands_Hornu.jpg/250px-B%C3%A2timents_du_Grands_Hornu.jpg)](/wiki/File:B%C3%A2timents_du_Grands_Hornu.jpg)

The *Cour centrale* was the focal point of the company town of [Grand-Hornu](/wiki/Grand-Hornu "Grand-Hornu").

Having bought the mining concession of [Grand-Hornu](/wiki/Grand-Hornu "Grand-Hornu") in 1810, French industrialist [Henri De Gorge](/w/index.php?title=Henri_De_Gorge&action=edit&redlink=1 "Henri De Gorge (page does not exist)") soon realized the need to accommodate the growing workforce of his expanding business. He commissioned architect [François Obin](/w/index.php?title=Fran%C3%A7ois_Obin&action=edit&redlink=1 "François Obin (page does not exist)") and, after his death, [Bruno Renard](/w/index.php?title=Bruno_Renard&action=edit&redlink=1 "Bruno Renard (page does not exist)"), to build a functional complex in a [neoclassical](/wiki/New_Classical_architecture "New Classical architecture") style. Grand-Hornu became one of the world's first purpose-built company towns. It was abandoned in 1954 after the mine was closed. It currently houses a museum of contemporary art and temporary exhibitions. Grand-Hornu is one of the four industrial sites in Wallonia that were listed by [UNESCO](/wiki/UNESCO "UNESCO") as [a World Heritage Site](/wiki/Major_Mining_Sites_of_Wallonia "Major Mining Sites of Wallonia") in 2012.

### Brazil

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Company_town&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Brazil")]

[Fordlândia](/wiki/Fordl%C3%A2ndia "Fordlândia") was established by American industrialist [Henry Ford](/wiki/Henry_Ford "Henry Ford") in 1928 as a prefabricated industrial town in the Amazon rainforest of Brazil. It was intended to be inhabited by 10,000 people, but it failed, and the city was abandoned in 1934.

### Canada

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Company_town&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Canada")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cf/Harmsworth_Hall_2.jpg/250px-Harmsworth_Hall_2.jpg)](/wiki/File:Harmsworth_Hall_2.jpg)

[Grand Falls-Windsor](/wiki/Grand_Falls-Windsor "Grand Falls-Windsor") was built as a company town in central Newfoundland

For more, see [List of company towns in Canada](/wiki/List_of_company_towns_in_Canada "List of company towns in Canada").

[Arvida, Quebec](/wiki/Arvida,_Quebec "Arvida, Quebec") was developed in 1927 as a company town. The town grew to have a population of about 14,000 inhabitants, four Catholic parishes, and many other denominations, parishes, and schools. It was known as "the City Built in 135 Days".[[15]](#cite_note-Peritz-15)

[Batawa](/wiki/Batawa "Batawa"), Ontario was set up by the Bata Shoe Company as a planned community around a shoe factory. The factory opened in 1939 and closed in 2000.

### Chile

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Company_town&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Chile")]

Two of Chile's [UNESCO World Heritage Sites](/wiki/UNESCO_World_Heritage_Site "UNESCO World Heritage Site") correspond to former company towns.

[Humberstone](/wiki/Humberstone_and_Santa_Laura_Saltpeter_Works "Humberstone and Santa Laura Saltpeter Works") was a settlement dedicated to the extraction of [saltpeter](/wiki/Potassium_nitrate "Potassium nitrate") during said commodity's boom in the late 19th and early 20th centuries. The town was founded and administered by [James Thomas Humberstone](/wiki/James_Thomas_Humberstone "James Thomas Humberstone")'s Peru Nitrate Company, later acquired by Compañía Salitrera de Tarapacá y Antofagasta (COSATAN), before turning into a [ghost town](/wiki/Ghost_town "Ghost town") by the 1960s due to the steady decline in the global price of nitrates caused by the development of chemically engineered fertilizers.

[Sewell](/wiki/Sewell,_Chile "Sewell, Chile") was a mining town intended as both a residential and industrial hub linked to the nearby [El Teniente](/wiki/El_Teniente "El Teniente") copper mine. Founded in 1906 by the [Braden Copper Company](/wiki/Braden_Copper_Company "Braden Copper Company"), it came to house over 16,000 inhabitants at its peak despite its remote location on the slopes of the [Andes](/wiki/Andes "Andes"). The problematic geography on which the town was erected led to its distinctive appearance, with steep staircases and no streets for vehicular access. Although the El Teniente mine remains active, the town itself began to be dismantled and abandoned by the late 1960s as the maintenance of a remote on-site town was no longer financially viable. Some industrial activities still occur on Sewell's grounds, but workers have primarily been relocated to nearby urban centres.

[Leverkusen](/wiki/Leverkusen "Leverkusen") was founded in 1861 around [Carl Leverkus](/wiki/Carl_Leverkus "Carl Leverkus")' dye factory that later became the headquarters of [Bayer](/wiki/Bayer "Bayer"). The city of [Ludwigshafen](/wiki/Ludwigshafen "Ludwigshafen") has been dominated by [BASF](/wiki/BASF "BASF")'s plants since the chemical company moved here in 1865. The neighboring municipality [Limburgerhof](/wiki/Limburgerhof "Limburgerhof") also emerged from housing estates for BASF workers. [Neuölsburg](/w/index.php?title=Neu%C3%B6lsburg&action=edit&redlink=1 "Neuölsburg (page does not exist)"), built since 1875 for the workers of [Ilseder Hütte](/wiki/Ilseder_H%C3%BCtte "Ilseder Hütte"), was a separate municipality until 1964. Many other companies, especially in the mining and steel industry, built housing estates for their workers near existing cities rather than separate company towns. A notable example is *Siedlung Eisenheim* in [Oberhausen](/wiki/Oberhausen "Oberhausen") from the mid-19th century, once home to 1200 steel smelters of the [Gutehoffnungshütte](/wiki/Gutehoffnungsh%C3%BCtte "Gutehoffnungshütte"), which is now protected as a historic monument. The mining settlements built around the coal pits of the [Ruhr](/wiki/Ruhr "Ruhr") region were called *Zechenkolonien* ("pit colonies").

[Wolfsburg](/wiki/Wolfsburg "Wolfsburg") was created as a planned city in 1938 to host the [Volkswagen plant](/wiki/Wolfsburg_Volkswagen_Plant "Wolfsburg Volkswagen Plant") and its workers. A similar Nazi-era planned town is [Salzgitter](/wiki/Salzgitter "Salzgitter"), built around the [Reichswerke Hermann Göring](/wiki/Reichswerke_Hermann_G%C3%B6ring "Reichswerke Hermann Göring") that later became the [Salzgitter AG](/wiki/Salzgitter_AG "Salzgitter AG"). In 1950s East Germany, [Eisenhüttenstadt](/wiki/Eisenh%C3%BCttenstadt "Eisenhüttenstadt") ("ironworks city", initially *Stalinstadt*) was built from scratch, housing the workers of the [Eisenhüttenkombinat Ost](/wiki/EKO_Stahl "EKO Stahl") ironworks.

Toyota city is the company town of [Toyota Motor Corporation](/wiki/Toyota "Toyota").

In Japan, an equivalent to company towns are "*Kigyō [Jōkamachi](/wiki/J%C5%8Dkamachi "Jōkamachi")*" (企業城下町 , Company [castle town](/wiki/Castle_town "Castle town")). However, these differ from company towns because the companies do not own or develop them. Instead, the term refers to towns where a specific company has a major influence on the town's economy due to that company or its subcontractors employing a significant part of the town's population.

Some company towns are named after their influential companies, such as [Hitachi, Ibaraki](/wiki/Hitachi,_Ibaraki "Hitachi, Ibaraki") (日立市) former name Sukegawa (助川), and [Toyota, Aichi](/wiki/Toyota,_Aichi "Toyota, Aichi") (豊田市) former name Koromo (挙母). Hitachi, Ibaraki where the "castle" company is the electric machine manufacturer [Hitachi](/wiki/Hitachi "Hitachi"); Toyota, Aichi where the "castle" company is the automobile manufacturer [Toyota](/wiki/Toyota "Toyota"). Hitachi City changed its name from Sukegawa City and named the area of the town where Hitachi's mother factory in 1 September 1939. Toyota City changed its name from Koromo City and named the area of the town where Toyota's headquarters is situated "[Toyota-machi](/w/index.php?title=Toyota-machi&action=edit&redlink=1 "Toyota-machi (page does not exist)") [[ja](https://ja.wikipedia.org/wiki/%E3%83%88%E3%83%A8%E3%82%BF%E7%94%BA "ja:トヨタ町")]" (トヨタ町, note that [katakana](/wiki/Katakana "Katakana") is used here to reflect the company name) in 1 January 1959.

### Mexico

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Company_town&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Mexico")]

During the [Porfiriato](/wiki/Porfiriato "Porfiriato"), American-owned [mining towns](/wiki/Mining_in_Mexico "Mining in Mexico") that settled mostly uninhabited areas included [Cananea](/wiki/Cananea "Cananea"), [El Boleo](/wiki/El_Boleo "El Boleo"), [Nacozari](/wiki/Nacozari "Nacozari"), [Navojoa](/wiki/Navojoa "Navojoa"), [Copala](/wiki/Copala,_Sinaloa "Copala, Sinaloa"), [Concordia](/wiki/Concordia_Municipality "Concordia Municipality"), [Santa Eulalia](/wiki/Santa_Eulalia,_Chihuahua "Santa Eulalia, Chihuahua"), [Santa Rosalía de Camargo](/wiki/Santa_Rosal%C3%ADa_de_Camargo "Santa Rosalía de Camargo"), [Batopilas](/wiki/Batopilas,_Chihuahua "Batopilas, Chihuahua"), and [Esperanzas](/w/index.php?title=Esperanzas_(town)&action=edit&redlink=1 "Esperanzas (town) (page does not exist)"). Americans and Mexicans were segregated,[[16]](#cite_note-16) with the 1906 [Cananea strike](/wiki/Cananea_strike "Cananea strike") precipitating the [Mexican Revolution](/wiki/Mexican_Revolution "Mexican Revolution").

### Namibia

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Company_town&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Namibia")]

See also: [Labour hire in Namibia](/wiki/Labour_hire_in_Namibia "Labour hire in Namibia") and [1971–72 Namibian contract workers strike](/wiki/1971%E2%80%9372_Namibian_contract_workers_strike "1971–72 Namibian contract workers strike")

[Oranjemund](/wiki/Oranjemund "Oranjemund") was privately owned by [De Beers](/wiki/De_Beers "De Beers") until 2017 and thus governed by the diamond mining company's administration. In 2011, the political administration was handed over to the [government](/wiki/Government_of_Namibia "Government of Namibia"), which proclaimed it a town. It is now governed by a seven-seat town council.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

During South African [Apartheid](/wiki/Apartheid "Apartheid"), a form of company towns existed under the contract labor system in Namibia, then [South West Africa](/wiki/South_West_Africa "South West Africa"), a colony of South Africa. Indigenous workers were coerced into signing yearlong contracts in caged-in factory compounds, which they were not allowed to leave without permission, with movement strictly controlled by [Pass laws.](/wiki/Pass_law "Pass law") In addition they lived in the compound dorms and primarily got food from the compound's cafeteria, both under the control of their employers.[[17]](#cite_note-Moorsom19792-17)[[18]](#cite_note-18) As such, alongside the typically bad working conditions, it has been characterized by many as close to slavery.[[19]](#cite_note-:52-19)[[20]](#cite_note-:02-20)

### Spain

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Company_town&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Spain")]

#### Industrial colonies in Catalonia

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Company_town&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Industrial colonies in Catalonia")]

See also: [History of the cotton industry in Catalonia](/wiki/History_of_the_cotton_industry_in_Catalonia "History of the cotton industry in Catalonia")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/10/Col%C3%B2nia_Pons.jpg/250px-Col%C3%B2nia_Pons.jpg)](/wiki/File:Col%C3%B2nia_Pons.jpg)

Cal Pons, a textile company town, or *industrial colony*, in [Puig-reig](/wiki/Puig-reig "Puig-reig")

[Catalonia](/wiki/Catalonia "Catalonia"), located in north-eastern Spain, has an especially high density of company towns, known locally as industrial colonies. They are especially concentrated in river basins along the [Ter](/wiki/Ter_River "Ter River") and [Llobregat](/wiki/Llobregat "Llobregat") and their tributaries. In [Berguedà](/wiki/Bergued%C3%A0 "Berguedà"), for example, within 20 km, there are 14 colonies. The total number in Catalonia is around a hundred. These were small towns created around a factory or mine, built in a rural area, and, therefore, separate from any other population. They typically housed between 100 and 500 inhabitants; in some cases, upwards of 1000 people lived in these towns.[[21]](#cite_note-Serra-21): 101

These industrial colonies were an emblematic aspect of industrialization in Catalonia, specifically, the second industrialization, which resulted in certain areas that were once purely rural becoming industrial. They were first created in the second half of the nineteenth century, especially in the 1870s after the American Civil War ended and raw cotton again became readily available. The last colonies were created in the early years of the twentieth century. Thus, company towns in Catalonia have a history going back some 150 years; however, in almost all cases, the company that operated the colony has now shut down. The most common industry in these colonies was textiles, and the most important from the standpoint of architecture, urban design, and the complexity of their operation. More than 75 textile colonies were recorded, although there were also mining, metallurgy, cement, and agricultural colonies.[[21]](#cite_note-Serra-21): 102

These colonies (particularly the textile-related ones) were mostly constructed close to a river. This was because they used hydraulic power (waterwheels) to run the factory. One reason for this was that Catalonia was poor in coal, and importing it was expensive. In addition, the Catalan rivers (with little volume but a very steep sloping run) provided free and almost inexhaustible energy, which was lacking only in times of drought.[[22]](#cite_note-clua-22)

The industrial colonies' system began to collapse in the 1960s due to their inflexible capital structure and social changes, such as the desire for workers to own appliances, cars, or homes, the declining influence of religion, and the opportunities offered by towns.[[21]](#cite_note-Serra-21): 118 The colonies gradually emptied of people even before the definitive industrial crisis, which worsened in 1978. In the 1980s and 1990s, almost all the factories in these industrial colonies closed. From that moment on, many colonies became towns that were now independent of the company, others were abandoned and remain without inhabitants. Other factories were leased to smaller industries or now lie empty.

Some of the more interesting colonies include: [Colònia Güell](/wiki/Col%C3%B2nia_G%C3%BCell "Colònia Güell"), in [Santa Coloma de Cervelló](/wiki/Santa_Coloma_de_Cervell%C3%B3 "Santa Coloma de Cervelló"), which contains several modernist buildings, such as the crypt church built by [Antoni Gaudí](/wiki/Antoni_Gaud%C3%AD "Antoni Gaudí"); [L'Ametlla de Merola](/wiki/L%27Ametlla_de_Merola "L'Ametlla de Merola"), [Puig-reig](/wiki/Puig-reig "Puig-reig"), where centenary traditional cultural activities are held, such as the representation of [Els Pastorets](/w/index.php?title=Els_Pastorets&action=edit&redlink=1 "Els Pastorets (page does not exist)"); the three colonies of [Castellbell i el Vilar](/wiki/Castellbell_i_el_Vilar "Castellbell i el Vilar"): [La Bauma](/w/index.php?title=La_Bauma&action=edit&redlink=1 "La Bauma (page does not exist)"), [El Borràs](/w/index.php?title=El_Borr%C3%A0s&action=edit&redlink=1 "El Borràs (page does not exist)"), [El Burés](/w/index.php?title=El_Bur%C3%A9s&action=edit&redlink=1 "El Burés (page does not exist)"), with buildings of architectural interest; [Cal Rosal](/wiki/Cal_Rosal "Cal Rosal"), between [Berga](/wiki/Berga "Berga"), [Avià](/wiki/Avi%C3%A0 "Avià") and [Olvan](/wiki/Olvan "Olvan"), which, in 1858, was the first colony in the era of building large colonies in the Llobregat; [Cal Vidal](/wiki/Cal_Vidal "Cal Vidal"), Puig-reig, which houses the Museum of the Vidal Colony, or the Museum of the Sedó Colony, ideal visits for anyone wishing to learn what life was like in one of these industrial experiments.

The mining city of [Kiruna](/wiki/Kiruna "Kiruna") in Sweden was originally around 1900, built by the mining company in an unpopulated area. Many buildings, including the church, were built by the company. Many smaller towns were built and even planned by companies. [Skoghall](/wiki/Skoghall "Skoghall") in [Värmland](/wiki/V%C3%A4rmland "Värmland") is such an example where [Stora AB](/wiki/Stora_AB "Stora AB") owned a papermill and contained a city-planning office.

### Ukraine

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Company_town&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Ukraine")]

The city of [Pripyat](/wiki/Pripyat "Pripyat") in Ukraine was established in 1970 solely to house the workers at the adjacent [Chernobyl Nuclear Power Plant](/wiki/Chernobyl_Nuclear_Power_Plant "Chernobyl Nuclear Power Plant") and their families.[[23]](#cite_note-23) It was one of several "[nuclear cities"](/wiki/Closed_city "Closed city") constructed by Soviet nuclear power firms throughout the latter half of the 20th century. All amenities, stores, and employment were conducted through each nuclear city's power plant administration.

[Coalwood, West Virginia](/wiki/Coalwood,_West_Virginia "Coalwood, West Virginia") is a company town as portrayed in the movie *[October Sky](/wiki/October_Sky "October Sky")*.

At their peak, there were more than 2,500 company towns, housing 3% of the US population.[[24]](#cite_note-:0-24) By the 1920s they were in decline due to factors [mentioned above](/wiki/Company_towns#Decline_of_American_company_towns "Company towns").

The rate of company owned housing however was higher, the [Bureau of Labor Statistics](/wiki/Bureau_of_Labor_Statistics "Bureau of Labor Statistics") surveyed a sample of 213 companies for 1916, a full 34.4% of their workforce lived in company housing. However, the survey covered primarily manufacturing (textile, iron & steel mills) and mining towns.[[25]](#cite_note-:1-25)

The companies that ran the towns were mainly labor-intensive companies such as coal, steel, lumber, and various war industries. Most people living in these towns were immigrants new to the country.[[24]](#cite_note-:0-24) The tight, [paternalistic](/wiki/Paternalism "Paternalism") control exerted by companies over the residents' behavior and even opinions caused issues and concerns, as was indicated [earlier](/wiki/Company_towns#Pullman_lesson "Company towns").[[26]](#cite_note-26)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/35/1922_New_England_Textile_Strike%2C_evicted_family%2C_Lonsdale%2C_R.I_%28July_15%2C_1922%29_01.jpg/250px-1922_New_England_Textile_Strike%2C_evicted_family%2C_Lonsdale%2C_R.I_%28July_15%2C_1922%29_01.jpg)](/wiki/File:1922_New_England_Textile_Strike,_evicted_family,_Lonsdale,_R.I_(July_15,_1922)_01.jpg)

It was not uncommon for families to be evicted from company owned homes during strikes such as this family shown living in a tent after being evicted during the [New England Textile Strike](/wiki/1922_New_England_Textile_Strike "1922 New England Textile Strike") in Lonsdale, R.I. - July 15, 1922

[Pullman, Chicago](/wiki/Pullman,_Chicago "Pullman, Chicago") was a company town created by [George Pullman](/wiki/George_Pullman "George Pullman"), the head of the Pullman railway company, to house his employees and their families.

[Segundo, Colorado](/wiki/Segundo,_Colorado "Segundo, Colorado"), was a company town where the [CF&I](/wiki/Colorado_Fuel_and_Iron "Colorado Fuel and Iron") coal company housed its workers. It offered adequate housing and promoted upward mobility through its sponsorship of a YMCA Center, an elementary school, some small businesses, and a [company store](/wiki/Company_store "Company store"). However, air pollution was a constant health threat, and the houses lacked indoor plumbing. As demand for metallurgical coal declined, the mine laid off workers, and Segundo's population declined. After a major fire in 1929, CF&I left town, and Segundo became a ghost town.[[27]](#cite_note-27)

The [Clearfield Bituminous Coal Corporation](/w/index.php?title=Clearfield_Bituminous_Coal_Corporation&action=edit&redlink=1 "Clearfield Bituminous Coal Corporation (page does not exist)") developed company towns in Pennsylvania.[[28]](#cite_note-28)

One famous company town was [McDonald, Ohio](/wiki/McDonald,_Ohio "McDonald, Ohio"), which was created by the [Carnegie Steel Company](/wiki/Carnegie_Steel_Company "Carnegie Steel Company") to house and serve the needs of its employees in the [Youngstown, Ohio](/wiki/Youngstown,_Ohio "Youngstown, Ohio"), area.[[29]](#cite_note-29)

[Marktown](/wiki/Marktown "Marktown"), [Clayton Mark](/wiki/Clayton_Mark "Clayton Mark")'s planned worker community, was an example in northwest [Indiana](/wiki/Indiana "Indiana").[[30]](#cite_note-30)

In the present-day United States, it is relatively rare for any place in which a single company owns all the property to be granted status as an [incorporated](/wiki/Incorporation_(municipal_government) "Incorporation (municipal government)") municipality. Instead, companies prefer their wholly owned communities to remain unincorporated, as this permits community administration to be carried out by appointed company officers rather than elected officials. However, incorporated municipalities heavily depend upon a single industry or organization and may be loosely considered a "company town", even though the company does not technically own the town.

[Scotia, California](/wiki/Scotia,_California "Scotia, California") is a company town, previously owned by The [Pacific Lumber Company](/wiki/Pacific_Lumber_Company "Pacific Lumber Company"), and is being dismantled through PLC's bankruptcy process.[[31]](#cite_note-Scotia,-31)

[Cass, West Virginia](/wiki/Cass_Scenic_Railroad_State_Park "Cass Scenic Railroad State Park"), is a former company town now a state park. Originally founded in 1901, the town of Cass served employees who cut and processed lumber from the surrounding mountain slopes.[[32]](#cite_note-WVSP-32) Some of the remaining houses are available for rental;[[33]](#cite_note-33) other features are the company store and an operating railway.[[32]](#cite_note-WVSP-32)

[Bay Lake, Florida](/wiki/Bay_Lake,_Florida "Bay Lake, Florida") and [Lake Buena Vista, Florida](/wiki/Lake_Buena_Vista "Lake Buena Vista") are controlled by [The Walt Disney Company](/wiki/The_Walt_Disney_Company "The Walt Disney Company").

[Bryce Canyon City, Utah](/wiki/Bryce_Canyon_City,_Utah "Bryce Canyon City, Utah") is a current company town incorporated in 2007, consisting solely of Ruby's Inn's property and its third-generation owners, the Syrett family.[[34]](#cite_note-34)

In 2021, the governor of Nevada, [Steve Sisolak](/wiki/Steve_Sisolak "Steve Sisolak"), announced a plan to launch so-called "Innovation Zones" in Nevada to attract technology firms. The zones would permit companies with large land areas to form governments with the same authority as counties, including imposing taxes, forming school districts and courts, and providing government services. The measure to further economic development with the "alternative form of local government" has not yet been introduced in the Legislature. Sisolak pitched the concept in his State of the State address on January 19. By allowing tech corporations to establish their governments, the plan is hoped to bring in new businesses at the forefront of "groundbreaking technologies" without the state cutting taxes or paying [economic rent](/wiki/Economic_rent "Economic rent") that previously helped Nevada attract companies like Tesla Inc.[[35]](#cite_note-35)

In March 2021, [Elon Musk](/wiki/Elon_Musk "Elon Musk") announced plans to incorporate the [Boca Chica](/wiki/Boca_Chica_(Texas)#Starbase "Boca Chica (Texas)") area of far southeastern Texas, the site of a [SpaceX](/wiki/SpaceX "SpaceX") [rocket manufacturing and launch facility](/wiki/SpaceX_South_Texas_launch_site "SpaceX South Texas launch site"), as the city of [Starbase](/wiki/Starbase,_Texas "Starbase, Texas"). Some have labeled the plans and SpaceX's existing operations in the area as an example of a company town.[[36]](#cite_note-36)[[37]](#cite_note-37) After voters approved a municipal ballot measure, formal incorporation of the city of Starbase occurred on May 20, 2025. Bobby Peden, a SpaceX vice president, is the first mayor of the city.
